data.title: Founder of Chinese property giant pleads guilty to fraud
data.description:
The founder of the failed Chinese property giant Evergrande is facing the threat of life in prison after pleading guilty to fraud and bribery.

Hui Ka Yan, also known as Xu Jiayin and once one of China’s richest men with a fortune of $45bn (£36bn), made the plea during a court hearing in Shenzhen earlier this week.

It marks a significant fall from grace for the businessman, who turned Evergrande into China’s biggest property developer before it collapsed under the weight of huge debts in 2021.

Evergrande’s unravelling has led to a raft of claims against Hui, including embezzlement of assets and illegal fundraising. The offences carry a maximum penalty of life imprisonment and confiscation of property.

The court in Shenzhen said it would announce its verdict on the case at a later date, claiming on Tuesday that Hui had “pleaded guilty and expressed remorse” during proceedings.

His trial took place after he was detained in China in Sept 2023 on suspicion of committing crimes. Hui, a former steelworker, has not been seen publicly since.

At its peak, Evergrande had a stock market valuation of more than £37bn, working on around 1,300 projects in 280 cities across China.

However, large debts crippled the company in 2021, sending the company’s value plummeting and sparking China’s property downturn, which has weighed on the economy for years.

The China Securities Regulatory Commission found in 2024 that Evergrande had inflated earnings and committed securities fraud.

The commission found that revenues were overstated by more than 560bn yuan (£60.6bn) by recognising sales in advance.

It fined Hui 47m yuan (£5.1m) and handed him a lifetime ban from participating in China’s stock markets.

The indebted property giant was fined 4.18bn yuan (£451.4m) before being ordered to liquidate by a Hong Kong court in 2024. The company was removed from the Hong Kong stock exchange last year.

The collapse of the company has fuelled a property crisis across China, as home sales slowed and construction companies scaled back developments.

Beijing has sought to prop up the property market by providing incentives to prospective home buyers.

However, the economy has since been dealt a fresh blow by the Iran war, straining China’s energy supplies and piling pressure on the country’s manufacturing sector.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Channel 4 programme chief quits amid shake-up at broadcaster
data.description:
Channel 4’s veteran head of programmes is to step down amid a leadership overhaul at the youth-focused broadcaster.

Ian Katz, the chief content officer, will leave in the autumn after nearly nine years in the role, making him the longest-serving programme boss in the channel’s history.

Mr Katz, a former BBC Newsnight editor and senior journalist at The Guardian, joined Channel 4 in 2018. He is credited with bringing shows such as Taskmaster to the channel, while he also oversaw the Russell T Davies drama It’s A Sin and documentaries including Russell Brand: In Plain Sight.

The 58-year-old was also responsible for driving Channel 4’s expansion on YouTube as the broadcaster tries to navigate a decline in traditional TV viewing and exodus of young audiences to streaming rivals.

However, Channel 4 has faced criticism in recent years that it has lost its edge and now relies too heavily on old formats such as Gogglebox and The Great British Bake Off. The channel was launched under Margaret Thatcher in 1982 with a remit to make challenging and provocative shows.

Mr Katz’s departure comes a month after Priya Dogra took over as chief executive of Channel 4. Ms Dogra was selected to replace Alex Mahon, who left last year after successfully fending off attempts to privatise the broadcaster.

The former Sky and Warner Bros executive will now seek to shape her new leadership team after a period of uncertainty for Channel 4, which lost both its chairman and chief executive last year.

Sir Ian Cheshire, the previous chairman, stood down at the end of his term last April and was appointed this month as the new chairman of regulator Ofcom.

Jonathan Allan, Channel 4’s former chief operating officer, who was in the running for the top job, also stepped down earlier this year.

Ms Dogra said: “Ian has been an outstanding creative leader for Channel 4 over nearly nine years – the channel’s longest-serving head of programming.

“He has overseen an era of creative renewal, delivering bold and distinctive public service programming with intellectual rigour, good humour and an unmistakably Channel 4 glint in the eye.”

The leadership shake-up comes as Channel 4 seeks to carve out its role in the streaming era. The broadcaster is spending less on making programmes as it grapples with declining advertising revenues from its traditional TV business.

Channel 4 has been striking closer partnerships with its rivals as public service broadcasters seek to build scale to fight back against the likes of Netflix. But it is also contending with a wider reshaping of the TV landscape, with Sky locked in talks to buy ITV’s broadcasting business.

Mr Katz said: “It’s been a privilege beyond words to lead Channel 4’s talented and passionate commissioning team through such a transformative period.

“I’m incredibly proud of what we’ve achieved, from pioneering the evolution of a commercial public service broadcaster into a digital streaming business to backing groundbreaking programmes and talent that have brought a bit of joy to audiences’ lives.

“Above all, I’m proud that Channel 4 has continued to be as disruptive and impertinent as ever, asking the questions and covering the stories that other broadcasters and streamers won’t.”

Mr Katz will remain in his role until October to commission and oversee the channel’s slate of programming for 2026.

data.title: Banking giants accused of links to Iranian money laundering
data.description:
HSBC and Standard Chartered have become embroiled in allegations of a multimillion-pound scheme to launder money for Iran, The Telegraph can disclose.

The two British multinational banks are accused of “unwittingly” processing payments which it is claimed may be linked to a complex sanctions-evading conspiracy.

They are among five banks – including JP Morgan Chase, Citibank and Bank of New York Mellon – which have been ordered by a United States judge to hand over their records for inspection.

The disclosure that major British banks have been linked to potential money laundering for Tehran comes as the White House threatens to punish any “foreign financial institutions” which support Iran.

Last week the US treasury department announced that it was “moving aggressively with Economic Fury, maintaining maximum pressure on Iran”.

In a statement, it said: “Financial institutions should be on notice that the department is leveraging the full range of available tools and authorities and is prepared to deploy secondary sanctions against foreign financial institutions that continue to support Iran’s activities.”

Relations between Sir Keir Starmer and Donald Trump have become increasingly strained over the Iran war. Last week, the US president threatened to rip up his trade deal with Britain after Rachel Reeves hit out at the “folly” of going to war without an exit strategy.

Mr Trump suggested that he could change the agreement he reached with Sir Keir last year, because of Britain’s lack of support in the Middle East.

According to documents filed in a US court case earlier this month, the Turkish branch of Kuwait Finance House (KFH), known as Kuveyt Turk, “used front companies to hide underlying Iran connections”.

It is alleged that Kuveyt Turk used five banks – HSBC, Standard Chartered, JP Morgan Chase, Citibank and Bank of New York Mellon – as “correspondent banks”, meaning they process transactions in dollars on their behalf.

Documents submitted to the court allege that a transaction processed by Kuveyt Turk for $5.7m (£4.2m) involved the National Iranian Oil Company, which has been sanctioned by the US, EU and Britain.

Elsewhere in the documents, submitted to the federal court for the southern district of New York, it is alleged that recent payments in euros for equipment, which may be related to nuclear weapons and ballistic missiles, have been processed by Kuveyt Turk for two US-sanctioned companies.

The two companies – Khatam Al-Anbiya Construction and Arvand Petrochemical Company – are affiliated with Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC).

Court documents allege that purchases processed by Kuveyt Turk relate to fan blades and a large axial fan, which are used in underground ballistic missile silos and “may be related to nuclear weapons and ballistic missiles”.

They go on to say: “Khatam Al-Anbiya Construction is one of the major arms of the IRGC, and is known for indirectly funding Hamas, Hezbollah, and the Iranian nuclear and ballistic missile industry.

“This is a complex money-laundering scheme involving payments by Iran to Kuwait-owned entities, including in US dollars. The evidence detailed above shows doctoring of records to hide sanctioned transactions.”

There is no suggestion that HSBC, Standard Chartered, JP Morgan Chase, Citibank and Bank of New York Mellon engaged directly or knowingly in sanction-evading activity, and no suggestion that they were involved in any euro-based transactions.

The details came to light in a case involving a dispute between Delaram Zavarei, an Iranian businesswoman, and KFH and its subsidiaries.

Ms Zavarei was a “vocal opponent” of the Iranian regime and openly criticised it on social media, court documents say. In 2002, she moved her family to Dubai and continued to manage her Iranian assets from there.

She claims that she was subject to a “politically motivated state-sponsored campaign” by the Iranian government, which also targeted her family and corporate interests.

Her lawyers allege that KFH and its subsidiaries “conspired” with Iran, through its state-owned entities, to steal millions of dollars worth of equipment from her oil and gas company, Energy Exploration and Development Company Ltd (ENEXD).

KFH and its subsidiaries were “rewarded handsomely” by Iran for “pursuing the conspiratorial vendetta” against ENEXD, court documents allege.

Lawyers acting for ENEXD argued that Kuveyt Turk, along with a second Turkish bank, was used by Iranian companies to process payments worth millions of dollars to KFH and its subsidiaries.

Ms Zavarei is preparing to launch a separate claim in the High Court in London, and her legal team has already issued a series of letters before action.

A legal expert in sanctions who is familiar with the case said: “My view from what I have seen is that each of the correspondent banks appear to me to have unwittingly become involved.

“I say unwittingly because some of these banks have already been fined hundreds of millions of dollars for processing Iranian payments and certainly wouldn’t want to continue to do so.

“Secondly, these corresponding banks, in my view, do things properly and carefully in order to comply with sanctions. Thirdly, all the corresponding banks will contractually prevent banks like Kuveyt Turk from making payments that emanate from or have any links to Iran.”

Georgina Halford-Hall, chief executive of Whistleblowers UK, is supporting Ms Zavarei. She said: “The reason that this case has taken so long to surface is not only because of the complexity and the number of different moving parts, but because British laws fail to provide for whistleblowers like Ms Zavarei and others in this case.

“They are frightened for their personal safety and afraid that some of these entities could exert pressure or retaliation on their families in other countries. If we are to combat financial crime, we need a UK whistleblowing framework to set proper standards.”

HSBC, Standard Chartered, JP Morgan Chase, Citibank, Bank of New York Mellon and KFH declined to comment. Following an objection by Citibank, ENEXD has been asked by the judge to file an additional request for discovery based on new information.

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”

data.title: How Sadiq Khan made London into a taxi driver’s worst nightmare
data.description:
It should be a straightforward journey that needn’t take too long – Liverpool Street to London Bridge station, two areas heavily populated with white-collar workers heading from the commuter belts into the capital for their careers.

However, those opting to drive the short one-and-a-half miles find themselves forced to go a more roundabout way – through Whitechapel, in order to avoid the bus gate at Bishopsgate, which prevents motorists (yet allows buses and bikes) from accessing the road.

So, instead of taking around five minutes in the car, it takes between 20 and 30 minutes. Realistically, it would be quicker for drivers to swap four wheels for two legs.

Bishopsgate is just one of the many roads in the capital that have become increasingly unwelcoming to motorists, with a recent survey conducted by TomTom, the satnav company, finding that London is the slowest-driving capital city in the world.

It takes an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to travel one measly kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of the city. The analysis also found drivers lost an average of 141 hours stuck in rush hour traffic in 2025.

This glacial pace is annoying when you are trying to get somewhere in a hurry, to attend an important meeting, perhaps, or to make it to the theatre before curtain call. However, for the black cab drivers of London, increasingly congested, slow and difficult-to-navigate roads are leading to a significant loss of earnings, and even putting their livelihoods – and the sight of the London icon – at risk of extinction.

As of late 2025, the number of licensed drivers had fallen to around 14,800, down from more than 22,500 a decade earlier.

For Steve Cox, who became a black cab driver in 2022, navigating the streets of London has become increasingly difficult. He cites low-traffic neighbourhoods (LTNs) – areas where roads are closed to traffic to allow cyclists and pedestrians to take priority – as one of the main contributors to the capital’s slow roads.

“What used to be a 15-minute journey can now take you up to an hour,” the 54-year-old explains. “Tottenham Court Road is severely restricted for motorists between 8am and 7pm, which are some of your peak hours as a black cab.

“If you want to go from one side of Tottenham Court Road to the other to drop a passenger, we have to do two different squares and go round several back roads. It’s just ridiculous.

“It’s another 15 to 20 minutes on the journey time, and probably another £15-£20 for the customer. I’m embarrassed to charge them. It’s unfair and leads to complaints that aren’t down to me – and results in fewer people trying to flag us down.”

While LTNs were initially implemented in London in the 1970s (although under a different name) to filter traffic in the Hackney area, they were implemented en masse in March 2020 under Mayor of London Sir Sadiq Khan’s plans to improve road safety, decrease air and noise pollution, and encourage “active travel” such as walking and cycling.

Now, it is thought four per cent of London’s nine-million-strong population lives in an LTN.

These have made navigating around the capital increasingly problematic; approximately 95 per cent of black cab drivers report that LTNs and poor road conditions make picking up and dropping off passengers difficult.

Cabbies also cite London’s new speed restrictions as a reason why the capital often remains at a standstill. According to Transport for London (TfL), more than half of London’s roads now have a 20mph limit. This includes all roads within London’s Congestion Charge zone, as well as major roads, such as the A107 corridor and the White Hart Roundabout.

While this was implemented to try and cut deaths and serious injuries, City Hall was previously told this change was “devastating to the taxi industry” by Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association in 2024.

“I understand the need for 20mph zones around schools,” says Frank O’Beirne, who has been a black cab driver for 14 years. “However, places like Park Lane, which have been reduced from three lanes of traffic to just one in order for there to be a bus and cycling lane, is just madness – especially now we’re stuck only being able to go 20mph there.”

The 50-year-old adds: “While our meters do go up in traffic, it’s only incremental – we need to be moving to actually have decent fares. Customers get so fed up in traffic they just jump out.”

Sadiq Khan’s green crusade for London has reached a nadir with the highly criticised and controversial ultra-low emission zone (Ulez). Since 2018, all newly-licenced black cabs need to be “zero-emission capable”, while the maximum operating age for older, more polluting diesel taxis has been reduced from 15 years to 12.

Cox says some older cabbies have been forced into an early retirement. “I know men in their 60s who have had their cab for 10 years and just didn’t see the point of carrying on investing so much money in a new car, and have gone and got jobs in Tesco instead.

“That and the stress of trying to earn enough money to keep you afloat is enough to push someone into another job. I don’t blame people for saying: ‘I don’t need this anymore.’”

It’s of little surprise, then, that a quarter of all black cabs on the roads before the pandemic are no longer in operation. Renting a traditional black cab, or Hackney carriage, is not cheap. Cox pays £360 a week to rent his vehicle, while others pay £400 and beyond. That doesn’t include the additional outgoings for cabbies, such as the mandatory two MOTs a year; while cab drivers don’t pay this, it does result in a loss of earnings as you can’t work while your vehicle is being serviced.

Cox says he needs to earn more than £3,000 a month just to break even on the investments he’s made to become a black cab driver. “It’s a stressful trade,” he says. “It’s made even more stressful by sudden roadworks, or ongoing protests by groups such as Palestine Action blocking roads. I can lose up to £300 a day in earnings when protests take place.”

Those still working as black cab drivers are now finding alternative methods to keep themselves afloat. O’Beirne works as a tour guide during slow periods. “I prefer doing out-of-town stuff now, because London just drives me mad,” he says. “Places like Tottenham Court Road, Mayfair, the City are just no-go areas. It’s a nightmare to navigate.

“Corporate gigs tend to pay better – I get a lot of American customers, who tend to be big tippers. But doing these gigs means you give up some freedom of setting your own hours, which is partly the beauty of doing this job.”

So, what could help London’s black cab drivers stay on the roads? For Cox, cutting, or at least reducing, some of the restrictions of LTNs on Hackney carriages would help with picking up and dropping off customers.

“If we really want to ease congestion, we could put a cap on private-hire drivers,” he adds. “They can operate without any restriction on the size of their fleet. I’ve heard of private-hire drivers getting licensed in Wolverhampton and then driving down here to get more work.”

Currently, private-hire vehicles vastly outstrip black cabs; TfL estimates more than 106,000 of these cabs are on the road.

Meanwhile, Steve Kenton, who has been a cabbie for 35 years, believes City Hall should listen more to black cab drivers. “We have some wonderful and intelligent people who choose to become black cab drivers,” he says. “We spend three to four years perfecting the Knowledge of London so we know the proper routes around the city. We attract individuals from every demographic. Our views and concerns deserve to be treated with more respect.”

“Fundamentally, if our wheels aren’t moving, we aren’t making money,” O’Beirne agrees. “We’re the only fully accessible transport network in London. Not everyone can ride a bike.”

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Sainsbury’s threatens to prosecute shoplifters caught swapping eggs
data.description:
Sainsbury’s has threatened shoplifters with police action if they are caught swapping premium eggs into cheaper boxes.

The supermarket giant has placed signs in stores telling customers that egg-swapping is “regarded as theft”, claiming that any footage of shoplifting will be shared with police.

In a picture posted on social media by Steve Dresser, a retail analyst, Sainsbury’s appeared to have put the signs under Burford Brown eggs, which are 75pc more expensive than the supermarket’s standard own-brand eggs.

A six-pack of Burford Browns eggs sells for £3.20 at Sainsbury’s, compared with £1.80 for its standard own-brand eggs.

Sainsbury’s told customers: “Footage of shoplifting will be passed to the police. We prosecute all shoplifters.”

The warning comes after shoppers complained they were returning home from supermarkets to find their expensive boxes of eggs had been filled with cheaper alternatives.

Videos emerged on social media earlier this year in which customers said they had been victims of egg theft.

Some claimed they had also realised “large” eggs had been swapped for “medium” ones after arriving home from the supermarkets.

Anita Wong, a shopper who sounded the alarm about egg-swapping in February, said she had been told by sales assistants that this was “not uncommon”.

Egg prices have risen from £2.48 for a dozen in early 2022 compared with £3.31 today, according to the Office for National Statistics, with the sector hit by higher costs.

However, supermarkets have suggested that cost of living pressures are not the biggest driver of higher shoplifting levels, claiming that organised criminals are hitting their stores periodically.

The Telegraph revealed last weekend that Asda was considering placing products such as razors and perfume in vending machines, so thieves could not target such products.

Some bosses claim retailers are not receiving enough support from police. Writing in The Telegraph earlier this month, Thinus Keeve, Marks & Spencer’s retail director, urged the Mayor of London to “prioritise effective policing”.

He said attacks were becoming “more brazen, more organised and more aggressive” across Britain’s high streets every day.

A Sainsbury’s spokesman said: “We have a range of security measures in our stores and if our managers notice specific items are becoming a target for theft, they may take additional measures in response.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: MI5 called in to protect Britain from breakthrough AI threat
data.description:
MI5 is racing to bolster the defences of Britain’s most critical companies against the hacking threat posed by a powerful new wave of AI.

Experts at the Security Service have been in contact with energy, water and communications companies to warn them about Mythos, an AI tool that has been deemed by its developer Anthropic to be too dangerous for general release.

A source said: “They [MI5] have been asking critical companies to be fully aware of the threat. They are making those responsible aware that the threat has evolved.”

Companies which own Critical National Infrastructure (CNI) include National Grid, BT, water companies and the nuclear power giant EDF. MI5 is responsible for protecting their security.

Kanishka Narayan, the AI minister, did not name names but confirmed that such companies had been put on alert about the AI tool, which was developed by the Silicon Valley giant Anthropic.

Mr Narayan said ministers had gained a “deep understanding” of Mythos via the AI Security Institute, an official testing lab, which has shared its findings with GCHQ specialists.

He said: “As a result of that deep understanding, the first thing we are able to do is protect critical national infrastructure and call private businesses as well. We were able to take action and we’ll continue to do that in future as well.”

The details of any measures taken are being kept secret. However, the work signals an escalation of the official response to Mythos. The Telegraph previously revealed talks between the Bank of England and critical companies in the financial sector.

The Telegraph also understands that Richard Horne, the chief executive GCHQ’s National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC), intends to address the risks at a security conference in Glasgow this week.

Officials have been working with the National Protective Security Authority, part of MI5 that is charged with protecting critical industries. Security sources said the latest advice was being shared with relevant businesses so they can shore up their defences.

‘Step up’

According to Anthropic, Mythos is capable of finding new vulnerabilities in computer networks with little human oversight. It has already found thousands of flaws that could open up devastating cyber attacks.

Anthropic has so far held back from a public launch of Mythos because of such fears. It has created an industry group, called Project Glasswing, made up of tech giants, including Apple and Microsoft, and Wall Street banks. The companies will have early access to the technology so they can fix any flaws they find.

The UK has also had access to Mythos through the AI Security Institute. The lab warned last week that Mythos was a “step up” on past AI and could undertake attacks that might normally take a human professional days of work.

However, companies have been privately clamouring for access to the tool. Pip White, Anthropic’s UK chief, said last week that British businesses would be invited to join Glasswing “in the very near term”.

Mr Narayan said it was important to pursue the opportunities created by the latest AI and protect systems from it.

He said: “We need to say this very seriously. We need to have the best frontier AI capability today.

“I’d encourage all firms to move both quickly and seriously in making sure that they’re protected, as well as making the most of the opportunities of AI adoption.”

Britain’s critical infrastructure owners will need to use Mythos to thoroughly test their own defences and uncover bugs that could be exploited by criminals. Banks including Barclays, Lloyds and NatWest have asked Anthropic for access.

Hackers previously targeted drinking water supplies, although they failed to cause any outages. In one Irish case, Iranian cyber-attackers knocked out supplies to one region for several days.

A government spokesman said: “The UK government is aware of the potential risks posed by emerging technologies and we remain vigilant against any potential threats.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Miliband to wipe out energy debt of poorest households in Iran bailout
data.description:
Poor households are in line to have their debts to energy suppliers paid down by taxpayers under plans being considered by Ed Miliband.

The Energy Secretary is in talks to soften the blow of the Iran conflict on family fuel bills with public funds, industry sources said.

The move is one option on the table as the Government grapples with ways to provide targeted support and avoid the costly blanket subsidies deployed in the Ukraine crisis.

Ofgem, the energy regulator, has already prepared a debt relief scheme that will forgive around £500m in debt accumulated by some 195,000 families during the 2022 energy crisis.

But the launch of that scheme has now been temporarily delayed while ministers consider expanding it to account for the conflict in the Middle East, The Telegraph understands.

It is not clear how such an expansion would be paid for.

Paying for debt relief

The cost of the existing £500m scheme is being spread across all households, with the average bill-payer expected to see £5 added to their bills by 2027-28 to fund the debt forgiveness.

Piling any further costs onto households is likely to be controversial, particularly given Labour’s pledge to cut bills by £300 a year by 2030.

Another way to pay for the debt scheme could be an increase in windfall taxes on renewable electricity generators, which Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, is expected to announce this week.

A spokesman for Ofgem declined to comment on Monday due to “purdah” rules ahead of May’s local elections.

There is growing concern across the energy industry about surging household debts, which officially reached £4.6bn last year but is expected to hit £7bn by the end of 2026 – more than triple 2022’s total.

Persistently high energy costs are largely to blame for the increase in debts, according to experts, although there has also been an uptick in people choosing not to pay.

The increase is problematic not only for those indebted to energy suppliers, but also for other households who have to pay an average surcharge of £50 a year to cover the overall shortfall.

Now, with the Middle East crisis sending fuel costs surging higher, there is concern that the debt mountain will grow larger as poorer families struggle to make ends meet.

Bills fell for millions of people this month when Ofgem’s energy price cap dropped from £1,758 to £1,641 a year.

But it is then expected to rise again to about £1,837 per year in July, following surges in the gas price, according to analysts at the consultancy Cornwall Insight.

This has prompted calls for a support package for households, although ministers have so far stressed that it will only be targeted at the poorest.

Ms Reeves has also warned against overreacting with “knee-jerk responses” that could worsen the Government’s finances in the long run.

‘Ready to go’

An industry source said the ease of expanding the existing debt relief scheme was that it was “ready to go,” already targeted at the vulnerable and could simply be adapted.

Some energy companies are nervous about the proposals for further debt relief amid concerns it will create a “moral hazard” and encourage people not to pay, while others are understood to be lobbying in support.

A person familiar with the scheme stressed that households would only be granted relief if they were engaging with their supplier and had agreed to a repayment plan.

Ofgem is also considering changes that would allow energy suppliers to cut off households who fail to register themselves within a fortnight of moving into a property.

Currently, when new tenants move in, accounts are switched to an anonymous “occupier” status and bills build up until the new homeowners contact the supplier.

The regulator said the changes aimed to prevent people from running up unpaid debts when they move house, as part of efforts to tackle bill-dodgers.

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “Tackling the affordability crisis is our number one priority. 

“Thanks to the Government’s action at the Budget, the energy price cap has fallen by 7pc in April, protecting households with lower bills until the end of June.

“We are working urgently with Ofgem to drive debt out of the system, including on its proposals for a potential debt relief scheme, and to deliver an energy market that puts people first and lowers bills for everyone.”

data.title: Reeves vows to unlock ‘tens of millions’ of barrels of North Sea oil
data.description:
Rachel Reeves has vowed to unlock “tens of millions” of barrels of oil and gas in the North Sea, just as Ed Miliband condemned fossil fuel exploration as an attack on the climate.

The Chancellor confirmed on Tuesday that offshore operators would be allowed to open new oil and gas fields near existing projects.

So-called “tie-backs” could “result in tens of millions more barrels of oil and gas being available for UK supply”, the Chancellor told the Commons on Tuesday, adding that the move would help to “support our energy security”.

She was speaking less than two hours after Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, declared that the “era of fossil fuel security is over” and said “clean energy is now the only route to financial security, energy security and indeed national security”.

The comments and timing reflect a growing tension within the Government over how best to manage the North Sea and its remaining reserves of oil and gas.

The North Sea Transition Authority – the government regulator – has said that up to 25 billion barrels of oil and gas could be recovered from UK waters if global prices and UK taxes were favourable.

Ms Reeves has made it clear that she supports further drilling in British waters, something Mr Miliband is ardently against.

Addressing a conference organised by the Good Growth Foundation in London on Tuesday, the Energy Secretary rejected calls to lift his ban on new exploration.

He said: “Drilling every last drop will not take a penny of bills because fossil fuels are sold on global markets ... New exploration licences, which we are ending, would make no material difference to capacity and therefore security of supply.

“Some people have said that despite the fact that new licences won’t lower prices and won’t give us energy security, we should issue them anyway – but in saying this, they would just ignore the reality of the accelerating climate crisis, which is the greatest long-term threat multiplier we face.

“Every piece of credible scientific evidence says that if every country took the approach and drilled every last drop, it would take us to global warming well past two degrees to three degrees and more.”

Mr Miliband faces one of his most controversial decisions to date: whether to approve the massive Rosebank oil field west of Shetland and the similarly huge Jackdaw gas field in the North Sea.

Both fields are owned by Adura – a new company jointly owned by Shell and Norwegian state energy giant Equinor, which have already invested billions.

In opposition, Mr Miliband described both projects as “climate vandalism” but in private meetings with the industry, Ms Reeves and Sir Keir Starmer have supported them.

Darren Jones, the chief secretary to the Prime Minister, who spoke at the Good Growth Foundation conference in Westminster shortly after Mr Miliband, gave guarded support to continued exploitation.

“The overall debate is: how do we transition ourselves into more home-grown energy? There is no point trying to push people to do that too quickly because it feeds into populist politics,” he said.

“As a consequence of that, we are just going to need gas for many, many years ahead and we may as well get the industrial benefits of getting that from the North Sea.”

data.title: British potteries are in trouble. Here are the everyday ceramics to buy and use
data.description:
News of Denby Pottery calling in the administrators has brought the challenges faced by the British ceramics industry into sharp focus. Rising costs surrounding employment and energy have left the 217-year-old business, which produces all of its collections from its site in Derbyshire, operating at a loss. It is looking for a new buyer and has reportedly cut its 500-plus workforce by 80.

Denby isn’t the only one feeling the pinch. Mike Raybould, the chief executive of Portmeirion Group, says: “Energy remains one of the most significant pressures on our industry. Ceramics production is inherently energy-intensive, so sustained increases in energy prices have a direct impact on the cost of manufacturing in the UK.” Portmeirion, which was founded in the late 1960s, produces 100,000 kiln-fired pieces a week from its factory in Stoke-on-Trent and is stocked in around 60 countries. “The ‘made in the UK’ backstamp carries a huge amount of value in the global market,” adds Raybould.

Public support for Denby has been mobilised with a #SaveDenby campaign, but it will take more than just a hashtag to save the business. Raybould notes that the ceramics sector has called on the Government to be eligible for energy support schemes such as the British Industry Supercharger, which benefits steel, metal and chemical producers.

Then there is the issue around skills and recruitment. Emma Bridgewater, which also produces its ranges in The Potteries, has an ageing workforce. “So many of our highly skilled makers are coming up for retirement. The industry hasn’t had an entry programme or apprenticeship programme since the 1980s,” says a spokesperson. That will change next month, when six new recruits join the inaugural Craft Apprenticeship Scheme, which covers every element of the ceramic manufacturing process.

Emma Bridgewater, which was founded in 1986, has amassed a 100,000-strong base of ardent fans. “All of our pottery is hand-signed, and some customers will collect a particular decorator’s work and come to meet them at the factory, so there’s a real connection,” adds the spokesman. “By investing in buying British pottery to eat your breakfast off, you’re supporting an industry-wide regeneration of skills and expertise in giving young people in a city an opportunity to develop a career. It really reaches deep.”

On that note, here are some of the best buys to elevate the everyday in the kitchen, all proudly made in the UK.

data.title: BP hails ‘exceptional’ oil trading during Iran war
data.description:
BP has hailed an “exceptional” performance at the start of the year after cashing in on surging oil prices during the Iran war.

The energy giant said it expected trading revenues to jump during the first three months of 2026 owing to “heightened volatility in crude oil, natural gas and refined products prices”.

This represents a significant rebound for BP after a weak performance in the last quarter of 2025, demonstrating how the energy shock unleashed by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is benefitting the world’s biggest oil and gas giants.

At least a fifth of the world’s oil and gas supplies rely on the crucial trade artery.

The International Energy Agency (IEA) said global oil demand dropped sharply following the outbreak of the Iran war amid soaring prices and a shortage of supplies from the Middle East.

Figures from the IEA’s monthly oil report showed that global demand was down 3.4pc in March. It is expected to decline a further 1.1pc in April to 100.4 million barrels a day, which would be the lowest level in three years.

The IEA warned that “demand destruction will spread as scarcity and higher prices persist”.

BP highlighted the impact of surging oil and gas prices in its latest update, noting that the average price of a barrel of Brent crude hit $81 in the first quarter, up from $63 in the final three months of the year.

The oil price peaked at nearly $120 last month, although it is now hovering around $100 after the US and Iran struck a two-week ceasefire last week.

Blow to financial results

BP said the rapid changes in market conditions were “expected to impact financial results”. It added that oil production and operations would be “slightly lower compared to the fourth quarter 2025” after its operations in the Middle East were affected by the conflict.

Around a fifth of BP’s overall oil production is generated in the Middle East, although it does not operate any refineries in the region.

Other oil companies have been caught up in the conflict after their refineries were damaged by drone attacks.

The conflict represents the first challenge for Meg O’Neill, who took over as BP’s chief executive earlier this month.

She became BP’s third boss in just two and a half years following the departure of Bernard Looney after failing to disclose romantic affairs with colleagues, while her predecessor Murray Auchincloss was ousted late last year.

Ms O’Neill told staff that BP will be simplified into two main business units, moving the company back to the way it was structured before its ill-fated attempt to invest in green energy in 2020.

She said that one division of the group will focus on oil and gas production, while another will cover BP’s refining, distribution and retail activities.

In line with its current strategy, Ms O’Neill said BP would continue to redirect billions of pounds from green energy projects towards oil and gas in an attempt to increase profitability.

Shell said last week that it expected its oil trading business to report a boost in earnings following the rapid increase in oil prices.

data.title: Reeves hands Rolls-Royce £600m to build mini-nukes in Britain
data.description:
Rolls-Royce has been handed a £600m loan from Rachel Reeves to help accelerate the development of Britain’s first mini-nuclear power plant.

The Chancellor’s National Wealth Fund confirmed details of the borrowing facility on Monday, which will be used to build three small modular reactors (SMRs) at Wylfa on the Welsh island of Anglesey.

The manufacturing giant was selected as the Government’s preferred partner for the project last year.

However, design work will now begin after Rolls-Royce struck an agreement with Great British Energy – Nuclear (GBEN), the quango set up to manage the mini-nuclear programme.

GBEN said the contract would unlock the next stage of the project, which aims to help commercialise nascent SMR technology.

It will provide Rolls-Royce with funds to complete detailed design work, including regulatory applications and planning approvals.

The £600m loan is in addition to £2.6bn that Ms Reeves allocated to the scheme during last year’s spending review.

Construction of the power plant itself is not expected to begin until the early 2030s, with the facility set to come online in the mid-2030s. But GBEN is understood to be carrying out ground preparation works now.

The scheme is eventually expected to create 3,000 local jobs in Wylfa, Anglesey, as well as another 5,000 supply chain jobs across the country.

Ms Reeves said: “This investment, along with vital financing from the National Wealth Fund, will strengthen our energy security, create skilled jobs and help to build a new generation of homegrown nuclear technology that will power our economy for decades to come.”

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, added: “At a time of global instability, this is a major milestone for Britain’s energy security.

“We are backing a British company to deliver our first small modular reactors – creating a generation of good jobs, driving growth and providing clean, home-grown power for decades to come.”

GBEN said the contract with Rolls-Royce stipulates that the company must hit various “milestones”, although it did not immediately clarify what those were.

The three mini reactors are expected to have a combined capacity of about 1.4 gigawatts, enough to power three million homes for more than 60 years.

SMRs will be constructed from “modules” built in factories and then transported to sites for assembly.

The idea is meant to ensure the plants can be produced efficiently at scale and more quickly than larger nuclear projects.

In the longer run, it is hoped their lower price tags will also make them more attractive to private investors, who have long viewed bigger plants as too expensive and risky to back without government support.

Major technology companies, including Google, Amazon and Facebook-owner Meta, are investing in potential SMR technologies in the hope they could eventually be used to supply power-hungry data centres needed to develop AI.

Rolls-Royce is also building six SMRs for the Czech government.

Simon Bowen, the chairman of GBEN, said: “This is an immense moment for the UK nuclear programme, our organisation and the industry as a whole.

“Today represents a true recognition of the efforts made across GBEN, Rolls-Royce SMR and Government to get to this point, and I’m hugely proud of the team for reaching this critical milestone.”

Chris Cholerton, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce SMR, added: “This contract unlocks the delivery of our first three units and brings certainty to the UK SMR programme.”

data.title: British banks to be given access to AI ‘too dangerous to release’
data.description:
British banks will be given access to the most advanced artificial intelligence hacking bot from Anthropic after it was deemed too dangerous to release to the public.

The Silicon Valley AI lab confirmed UK financial institutions would be added to its Project Glasswing programme, through which companies are given early access to Anthropic’s Claude Mythos AI tool to stress-test their cyber defences.

Pip White, Anthropic’s UK chief, said the scheme would expand to British businesses “in the very near term, in the next week”.

She told Bloomberg Television: “As you would expect, the engagement that I have had from UK CEOs in the last week has been significant.”

A source familiar with the programme said they expected several UK businesses to sign up to the second phase of the project.

Banks and technology companies have been racing to shore up their cybersecurity systems after Anthropic revealed the capabilities of its Mythos bot. The AI software could autonomously find previously unknown bugs in software that could expose companies to devastating cyberattacks.

Anthropic said its AI tool had found flaws in every major technology operating system and internet browser faster than would be possible for a human hacker.

The $380bn (£281bn) company has delayed the launch of the tool to the public, because of its powerful cyber capabilities. It has so far released Mythos to a select group of tech giants, including Apple and Microsoft, as well as US banks such as JP Morgan, so that they can strengthen their security.

The Telegraph revealed last week that the Bank of England was preparing to warn City chiefs about the powerful new AI technology in upcoming security briefings.

The UK’s AI Security Institute, the Government’s testing lab, confirmed this week it had trialled the new technology, warning it was “a step up over previous frontier models”. The AI can link together cyberattacks that require multiple steps, similar to a human hacker, in a job that would typically take professionals “days of work”.

Ms White said Anthropic had delayed the release of Claude Mythos and put its “own limitations around this product because we know how powerful it can be”.

“If we have to slow down the release for the safe and responsible development of AI and that has a commercial impact, we will do that.”

The disclosure came as Anthropic confirmed it would be reinforcing its UK presence, opening a larger London office early next year with space for 800 people.

Anthropic has about 200 staff in London. The AI lab recently raised $30bn in new funding to expand and has been linked with an initial public offering as soon as this year.

The London expansion was revealed as Anthropic grappled with a rift with the White House. The US department of defense labelled Anthropic a “supply chain risk” after it refused to approve its technology for use in fully autonomous weapons.

Anthropic has sued the White House over the blacklisting, which would force US government departments to stop using its AI tools. The case is ongoing. UK government officials have attempted to woo Anthropic to expand its London presence amid the split with Donald Trump’s administration.

Ms White said: “London is already one of our most important research and commercial hubs outside the US, and our expansion in the knowledge quarter gives us the room to grow into.”

Separately, the Government confirmed on Thursday that a new £500m taxpayer-backed AI fund has made its first investment in a start-up that is developing powerful semiconductor technology. Callosum is seeking to compete with tech giant Nvidia.

The Sovereign AI Unit, chaired by technology investor James Wise, was launched at an event on Thursday evening. The fund will back early-stage British AI businesses to accelerate their growth.

Kanishka Narayan, the AI minister, told The Telegraph that the investments would help to build “deep relationships” with AI founders “to get companies to stay here over time” rather than relocate to Silicon Valley.

Mr Narayan said the Government was seeking to be a “much more active, strategic state” by becoming directly involved in early-stage start-ups. He said this would mean “rolling our sleeves up, helping these companies become a massive set of British AI successes”.

data.title: Labour blames OpenAI’s cash struggles for data centre cancellation
data.description:
A Labour minister has claimed OpenAI’s “challenging” financial situation is behind the decision to halt data centre investments in the UK, rejecting claims that high energy costs are to blame.

Kanishka Narayan, the AI minister, said that the ChatGPT developer dropped out of the project, called Stargate UK, because of its “internal financing context”.

This marks a split with OpenAI, which singled out the cost of energy when pulling the plug on an AI data centre in the North East last week.

The AI lab said it would revisit the plans, announced in September, when “the right conditions, such as regulation and the cost of energy, enable long-term infrastructure investment”.

However, Mr Narayan has now appeared to reject OpenAI’s comments.

Speaking on The Rest is Money podcast, he said there had been “no change to energy pricing, and there has been no change to the regulatory environment”.

“What has changed is, as is well publicised, OpenAI’s internal financing context has become more challenging and they have made similar calls in the United States with their programme there as well,” he said.

Britain has the highest industrial electricity prices in the developed world, although UK energy costs have fallen slightly since OpenAI unveiled plans for Stargate UK.

Data centres, and particularly AI systems, are energy-intensive, making them vulnerable to high electricity costs.

OpenAI confirmed this month that it has raised $122bn (£97bn) in new funding and the business is believed to be targeting a $1tn public float as soon as this year.

But it also burned through an estimated $14bn in 2025. This has led the company to cut back on costly projects, such as scrapping Sora, its expensive AI video app.

OpenAI, which is valued at $830bn, revealed its $500bn Stargate data centre investment plan at a White House press conference in January last year.

The project has since been beset by delays, while OpenAI has retreated from some of its other data centre ventures.

In Norway, OpenAI pulled out of another data project this week. It confirmed it would no longer rent AI computing power directly from the 100pc clean energy data site and instead lease capacity from Microsoft.

Since December, OpenAI’s international expansion efforts have been led by George Osborne, the former Conservative chancellor.

In the US, OpenAI also cancelled an expansion plan for its primary data facility in Abilene, Texas.

OpenAI declined to comment.

data.title: Why housebuilders have given up on London
data.description:
London is not building. In the first quarter of this year, 23 of the capital’s 33 boroughs started work on precisely zero new homes. How has a city, notorious for rising rents and housing shortages, ended up in this position?

London’s social housing waiting lists are at their highest level in more than a decade, with 336,000 households registered – a quarter of the national total.

The average first-time buyer in the capital faces a price tag of £500,000, while private rents have risen two thirds in the past 15 years to an average of £2,271 a month. This is 65pc higher than the national average, according to the Office for National Statistics.

And yet the industry that could boost the supply of housing and ease the pressure has all but downed tools.

Several forces are fuelling the problem: a labyrinthine and laughably complex planning system, a building safety regulator that spent years delaying applications, tax changes that have decimated investor returns, and the end of a government scheme to help first-time buyers get on the ladder.

Private sector housing starts in the capital have plummeted 84pc since 2015, according to data compiled by consultant Molior London. Just 5,547 private homes were started in London last year, against a target of 88,000, and compared to almost 34,000 in 2015.

London is supposed to deliver 440,000 of the Government’s proposed 1.5 million new homes this Parliament. At current rates of delivery, that target looks fanciful.

A Kafkaesque planning nightmare

London’s planning environment is the most challenging in the country. Of the 10 local authorities in England with the worst approval rates for residential planning applications, seven are in London.

The capital operates under not one, but three layers of planning oversight: national policy, the London Plan, and 33 separate local authorities, each with its own requirements, its own politics and its own interpretation of what a new development should deliver.

Paul Rickard, chief executive of Pocket Living, an affordable housing developer active in the capital, describes the level of red tape faced by schemes his company submitted recently.

By the time the application hit the planning portal, it ran to 89 documents, cost £1m and required 27 consultants. A recent project in Hackney generated 9,000 pages of planning documents.

Rickard argues that planning requirements should be tailored much more closely to the size and nature of projects to avoid unnecessary costs and bureaucracy.

“If you’re doing a six-storey building in central London, why do you need an aviation study?” he asks. “There is no point in spending the money. Planes are not going to crash into this building.”

For a small developer, this is not a straightforward hurdle to clear. A failed application can run to seven figures, and a quarter of smaller schemes must go through two or three planning applications.

“How many small developers can afford a million quid and then go, ‘well, that’s okay, I’ll just try again?’” Rickard asks.

Throw in vocal Nimby movements across the capital, and the result is a politically difficult climate to build new homes in. Council majorities in London are narrow – sometimes built on a 19pc share of the vote – and opposition to development from existing residents tends to be louder than support from those who would benefit from new homes being built.

A new regulator and a fresh wave of delays

Into an already Kafkaesque environment, the Building Safety Regulator (BSR) arrived in 2022. While its purpose – to prevent another Grenfell – was impossible to dispute, its execution has left the sector reeling with costly delays.

At its worst, the BSR was rejecting more than two thirds of pre-construction applications and taking an average of 36 weeks to process those it approved, against a target of 12 weeks.

Of 193 new-build high-rise applications submitted since the regulator’s founding, just 15 had successfully cleared the process by the time of publication of a Telegraph Money report in October 2025.

For London, where the density of homes means that the BSR’s scope is particularly broad, the effect on new starts has been severe.

However, since The Telegraph’s damning report on the BSR, there has been progress. New leadership has overhauled internal processes and Rickard now regularly hears of approvals coming through in 12 to 14 weeks. Developers have begun adjusting their financial assumptions accordingly.

“A year ago, they might have said, ‘I need to budget 12 months for [one step of the process] because I just do not know,’” he says. “They no longer need to do that.”

But three years of backlog cannot be undone in months, and Kate Butler, policy director at the British Property Federation, points out that despite improved approval times, there remains an underlying uncertainty among investors and developers.

A regulator that has demonstrated it can take a year or more to respond – even if it now does so faster – leaves lingering doubt for investment decisions.

An unattractive environment for investors

Tax changes and regulation have made matters worse. Previously, an investor buying a block of 10 flats was able to pay stamp duty based on the average price of each unit, rather than the total value of the entire building, thanks to multiple dwellings relief.

But in 2024, the Treasury scrapped this, and now a developer buying a £5m block of flats is taxed as if they are buying one £5m mansion, pushing them above the highest stamp duty threshold. This single change wrecked the viability of many London housing projects, wiping billions from the sector overnight.

The Renters’ Rights Act and the abolition of Section 21 “no-fault” evictions have also introduced fresh uncertainty about underwriting rental income, as tenants gain the right to challenge increases through a tribunal system that is already under strain.

“How you’re able to underwrite or predict any rental growth that you’re going to get is another [challenge],” says Butler.

Underlying all of this is a land market that refuses to correct.

In theory, when the cost of building goes up, landowners should drop their asking prices to keep development viable. In practice, those who don’t need to sell simply don’t – they hold on for better prices, or pass the land down to the next generation.

Pocket Living ran the numbers on what land would need to cost today for it to be able to build on the same financial terms as in 2020. Accounting for doubled finance costs, rising build prices and new regulatory levies, the answer was a 78pc fall in land values.

In reality, Rickard says that prices have dropped by around 40-50pc. While this is a meaningful correction, it is still far short of what developers need to make the sums work.

Inadequate support for first-time buyers

For the first time in 60 years, there is no government first-time-buyer support scheme. This is yet another obstacle for developers because it is much more difficult for aspiring buyers to purchase homes.

Mortgage rates have also jumped in recent years, making it even harder to borrow for a home purchase, and London rents have risen to levels at which saving a deposit is, for many working Londoners, simply out of reach.

Mortgage income multiples, set in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis, were not designed for a housing market in which rents in many parts of London exceed equivalent mortgage payments.

“We have people going from paying £1,500 a month in rent, all in, to owning their home for £1,400 a month,” says Rickard. “It is not necessarily that people cannot afford to buy. It is that they cannot access buying. That is a subtle but very important difference.”

Without buyers, developers cannot sell, and without sales, there is little incentive to build. Developers overwhelmingly argue that if first-time-buyer support were restored, construction activity would swiftly increase.

Pro-development Government fuels optimism

It is worth noting that the current Government did not create this crisis. Much of what is broken was done before it took office, and both Butler and Rickard are broadly supportive of Labour’s ambitions: major planning reforms, pro-development messaging and an ambitious new homes target.

“They are the most pro-development and open government for well over a decade,” says Rickard.

But a housing crisis of such a scale requires more than goodwill and posturing; Rickard would like to see a more aggressive removal of the countless layers of cost and bureaucracy.

“We need to be bolder, and we need to do it more quickly,” he adds.

When asked if Labour is on track to hit its new homes target, Butler is diplomatic.

“I think it’s optimistic and ambitious,” she says. “It’s hard to tell how close they’ll get or if they will hit it in this Parliament. But I do think there are things that they can do, and if they do them with urgency, they will see a big increase in development.”

data.title: I visited Britain’s most battered train bridge. No wonder the railways are in chaos
data.description:
For people living near Watling Street in Hinckley, it’s not unusual to be woken in the night by a thunderous bang as another lorry comes to grief at the local railway bridge.

This small Leicestershire town is home to Britain’s most battered bridge. Surrounded by depots of enormous vehicles, it was struck at least 22 times by lorries and buses throughout 2024 and 2025.

Across the country, a railway bridge is hit by a road vehicle every five hours, with about 1,700 strikes on bridges recorded by Network Rail between April 2024 and March 2025.

The result was 120 days of delays to train journeys last year and a £23m bill for taxpayers, with each strike costing about £13,000.

Some 43 per cent of lorry drivers admit to not measuring their vehicle before setting off and 52 per cent do not take low bridges into account.

Outside Hinckley on the A5, heavy goods vehicles of all types, from concrete mixers to delivery vans, push their luck at speed, zooming eye-wateringly close to the underbelly of the bridge that bears the scars of past collisions.

Each time one crunches underneath the steelwork, mayhem ensues, with long traffic jams, train delays and debris littering the road, according to residents.

“It’s a nightmare,” one neighbour says. “It’s frightening, like a bang or an explosion. It happens all the time, at least once a week.”

The bridge’s luminous yellow-striped warning signs are visibly scarred by scrapes, while the supporting walls are missing large chunks of brick.

In the lay-by, splinters and vehicle parts lie scattered through the undergrowth and residents of a nearby caravan park report tables, chairs and even mattresses flying out of lorries.

A mother with two young daughters sighs as she recalls how the bangs shake the caravan almost weekly.

Chantelle Callton, 35, tells The Telegraph: “Oh my God, it shakes. It’s like a bomb’s gone off. Everybody speeds down here, so it’s very dangerous.”

The bridge is so notorious that she was warned about it more than six years ago before she moved there.

“If we go a week when it’s not been hit, we’ll say: ‘We’re due one soon,’” she adds.

Dianna Smith, 60, who runs the caravan site with her husband, says her son and his friend had to help a trapped driver out of a lorry in the night. “It’s a ticking time-bomb until something bad happens,” she adds.

“They get that much speed up and don’t realise they’re too high. One lorry’s top was pulled back like a tin opener. They have to stop the trains and it messes everything up.”

Betty Watton, 44, sitting outside with her two chihuahuas, lives closest to the bridge and says drivers often flee the scene.

She says: “One lorry tore the whole top off and just left it in the road, rolled up like a big piece of scrap. One time paint tins spilt and paint went everywhere, but it’s mainly parcels.”

At a nearby depot filled with HGVs and lorries, workers clad in orange hi-vis laugh knowingly at the mention of the bridge.

They refuse to speak, but one jokes to another: “How many times have you hit it?”

Vehicles over a certain height at the Greenway Environmental depot have to take lengthy detours to avoid the bridge, including driving on to the motorway, the assistant manager says.

Another worker suggests it might not be the most strategic location for a lorry depot.

“Earlier this year, one was full of papers, cardboard and packaging. When that got ripped apart, it peeled the lid back and paper and card flew everywhere, blocking the street off for quite a while,” he says.

“There are a lot of industrial sites and major warehouses here. The kind of places that have drivers that are coming here for the very first time. They follow their sat nav, but still...

“There are a lot of light-up signs the whole way saying: ‘High vehicle. Turn back.’ But it’s become quite an issue that lorry drivers use their phones while driving.”

Mountpark, which is funding the project and working with National Highways, has planned to lower the road under the bridge since 2023, but critics of the scheme fear it would be complex and lead to flooding.

Karen, the landlady of a nearby pub, says road works will only add to the traffic jams blighting her business.

With the construction of a new supermarket depot around the bridge, she fears that the problem will not be going away anytime soon.
