data.title: Transport watchdog director investigated over train ticket expenses
data.description:
Britain’s taxpayer-funded transport watchdog is investigating a member of its board over allegations he wrongly obtained student railcard discounts and double-claimed travel expenses.

Transport Focus, which represents passengers and is funded mainly by a grant from the Department for Transport, is reviewing payments to Rob Wilson, a non-executive director at the watchdog and a former Tory minister. The Cabinet Office is also investigating his expenses.

Records released under the Freedom of Information Act and seen by The Telegraph show that Mr Wilson claimed the cost of two journeys from both Transport Focus and one of his other publicly funded employers.

In November last year, the records show Mr Wilson claimed a £71.30 train ticket and £26 of parking from both Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission, a quango sponsored by the Cabinet Office, where he is deputy chairman.

He told each organisation he was travelling for a separate event related to his work for them. Mr Wilson reported to Transport Focus that the expenses were related to a board meeting and to the Social Mobility Commission that they were to attend a “higher education summit”.

The records show he submitted the same picture of the receipt for the Reading to London return ticket for both claims.

Mr Wilson also claimed twice for a £68.20 Reading to London return ticket and £26 parking charge in September 2024. On this occasion, he charged the expenses to Transport Focus and the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), an organisation funded from water bills, where he is chairman.

Again, Mr Wilson gave each organisation different reasons for the expenses. He told Transport Focus that he had travelled for a board meeting and the CCW that he had a meeting with the chairman of Anglian Water.

The Telegraph last month reported on Mr Wilson’s expenses records at the CCW, which showed he had obtained discounts on two first-class train tickets worth a third of their cost using a 16-25 railcard.

The discount brought the cost of first-class tickets under the cost of standard class, making them allowable under the CCW’s expenses policy. The organisation said the former MP, who is 61, had selected the option by mistake in two instances.

His expenses records at Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission indicate he made similar errors in his other roles.

He billed Transport Focus for £38.29 last April after booking two journeys with a 16-25 railcard discount. The same month, he also charged the Cabinet Office, which sponsors the Social Mobility Commission, £101.49 for three discounted journeys.

Transport Focus, which last year paid Mr Wilson a fee of £14,000, confirmed this weekend that his expenses claims are under investigation. Claiming a railcard discount fraudulently is a criminal offence and attracts a fine of up to £1,000.

A spokesman said: “We have a process ongoing which reviewed information we received in late March. The process is continuing, reflecting information that has emerged more recently. It would not be appropriate to comment further until that work is complete.”

A Cabinet Office spokesman said: “We were unaware of a duplicate claim by Mr Wilson with Transport Focus until the issue was raised by The Telegraph. We will be investigating this further.”

Lawyers for Mr Wilson said the railcard discounts again “arose from a mistake in his use of the relevant booking app” and its repetition was the result of a default setting.

They added that his double claiming was accidental and that he had intended to split the cost between his employers. Mr Wilson’s lawyers said he was investigating the matter and would ensure that any overpayment was reimbursed.

Mr Wilson, who left Parliament in 2017, also had difficulty selecting the right options while booking flights, according to his Transport Focus records. In 2020 and 2023, he booked and expensed British Airways flights as the Honourable Robert Wilson and The Right Honourable Robert Wilson.

The former honorific is typically used by sitting MPs to refer to each other in parliamentary debates but otherwise mostly applied to the children of viscounts. The Right Honourable is reserved for senior peers and sitting MPs who are members of the Privy Council, on which Mr Wilson never served.

Mr Wilson’s lawyers said the title was mistakenly added to his flight bookings by an administrator. He “had since engaged with British Airways to ensure that this error is corrected”.

It is not the first time that Mr Wilson has encountered controversy over his expenses. In the MPs’ expenses scandal, he was revealed to have claimed nine pence from taxpayers for a 352-yard car journey. He defended the claim, saying the cost of travel “does add up”.

data.title: Losses hit £53m at The Sun as it battles falling tabloid sales
data.description:
The Sun has reported a £53m loss as Rupert Murdoch’s newspaper battles falling tabloid sales.

Newly filed accounts for News Group Newspapers (NGN), which publishes The Sun and Sun on Sunday, show pre-tax losses widened from £18m in the year to July 2025.

It came as turnover fell by 7pc to £273m, which bosses blamed on a drop in print sales and algorithm changes that dented online readership.

The figures underscore the challenge facing The Sun, which has been grappling with how to restore profitability across its advertising-funded business model.

The Sun introduced a premium model last year, offering readers access to exclusive stories and columns by star writers such as Jeremy Clarkson for £2 a month.

The move, which mirrored a similar partial paywall launched by MailOnline, was aimed at reducing the title’s reliance on dwindling ad revenues.

However, the rollout of AI tools in Google’s search engine is posing a fresh threat to free-to-access online journalism, with a number of publishers already reporting sharp reductions in traffic.

The tabloid has also sought to offset declining readership by expanding in the US, while it has also made redundancies to cut costs.

The publisher employed 530 jobs at the end of the last financial year, down from 552 a year previously.

The Sun reported increased legal costs related to the phone-hacking scandal at The News of the World, which more than doubled last year to £36.7m.

This is thought to include a £10m settlement with the Duke of Sussex, which was struck with NGN last year. The publisher issued an apology for “serious intrusion” by The Sun and phone hacking by private investigators working for the News of the World.

The troubles at The Sun stand in contrast to Mr Murdoch’s broadsheet titles, which operate under a subscription model.

Revenues at The Times and Sunday Times increased to £390m, driven by growth in subscribers and advertising. The newspaper has been seeking to boost reader numbers by reducing the total number of stories it publishes, in a strategy it claims should result in “better stories”.

Decline of TalkTV

Separate accounts showed Mr Murdoch had lost more than £150m on his failed news channel TalkTV in a rare setback for the media mogul.

TalkTV, the opinionated broadcaster that was pulled off air two years ago, posted an operating loss of £8.7m in the year to July 2025.

It takes cumulative losses since the channel’s launch in 2022 to more than £150m. Mr Murdoch’s News Corp also booked a £25m writedown on its investments in the UK, which it said was in response to challenging market conditions affecting its broadcasting operations.

A £154m loan to TalkTV has been waived and the channel’s parent company has now ceased trading.

The demise of TalkTV marks a rare defeat for the nonagenarian tycoon, who last year handed control of his news empire to his eldest son, Lachlan Murdoch, after a bitter succession battle.

The deal will secure the Right-wing stance of Mr Murdoch’s outlets, which include Fox and the Wall Street Journal as well as his UK newspapers. Lachlan is closely aligned with his father’s politics, whereas siblings James, Elisabeth and Prudence are viewed as more liberal.

Mr Murdoch was separately handed a boost this week as a US judge dismissed Donald Trump’s $10bn (£7.4bn) lawsuit against The Wall Street Journal for publishing a lewd birthday note sent by the president to Jeffrey Epstein.

A spokesman for News UK said: “News UK is a portfolio of assets, each at a different level of digital maturity. 

“Those brands are managed as a whole and our investments are focused on formats which will deliver revenues over the long term to create a sustainable future for all of our journalism.”

data.title: Billionaire Weston family to spin off Primark
data.description:
The billionaire family behind Primark is to spin off the fashion chain from its food business, breaking up one of Britain’s biggest consumer conglomerates after 65 years.

Associated British Foods (ABF) said it planned to split Primark off by the end of 2027, paving the way for a separate listing on the FTSE 100.

ABF is listed on the London Stock Exchange but around 60pc of its shares are owned by the Weston family’s Wittington Investments. The conglomerate is one of the world’s biggest sugar producers and also owns brands including Ovaltine, Hovis and Twinings.

Analysts have predicted that a separate listing of Primark, which has 486 stores across 19 markets, could see it valued at a similar level to rivals such as Next, which is worth more than £16bn.

George Weston, the chief executive and a member of the controlling Weston family, will become chief executive of the food business, while Eoin Tonge will continue to lead Primark who took over as interim chief executive last year.

Mr Weston said there were “a lot of good things that will come from this separation”, adding that Primark was “very capable of standing on its own two feet” and would benefit from having a board of non-executives who had specific retail experience.

He said ABF’s food business, meanwhile, had been “under appreciated because most of the interest has inevitably been around Primark”.

Plans to press ahead with the separation come despite warnings from ABF that the war in Iran was likely to hit consumer spending.

Mr Weston said there was a “risk to Primark sales if the conflict persists and consumer spending deteriorates”.

“Based on the last couple of weeks, we’ve seen sales right across Europe reduce, but we just don’t know,” he said. “Some of our customers may stop shopping, but some other customers might start shopping at Primark because they see value there.”

Mr Weston said inflation from the Middle East conflict was likely to be “manageable” this year. ABF had locked in energy prices in advance, helping to limit the impact of the shock.

By July and August, though, he said ABF would “become increasingly exposed to the higher costs, should they still be in place”.

The situation “might resolve itself before those extra costs come through the system”, he added.

Mr Weston said ABF had not yet seen any disruption to shipments. One vessel carrying its goods passed through the Suez Canal last weekend. Some freight companies have been avoiding the Egyptian waterway over fears of spillover from the conflict nearby.

Shares in ABF slumped as much as 5pc to £17.90 on Tuesday. Analysts at Barclays said this was likely driven by the “cautious commentary” about Primark sales, as well as weaker-than-expected profits in its sugar business.

ABF posted a 2pc fall in revenue to just under £9.5bn in the 24 weeks ending February, while profit declined by 9pc to £632m.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: World’s biggest condom maker warns of price rises because of Iran war
data.description:
The world’s biggest producer of condoms is preparing to raise prices by almost a third after the Iran war sent costs soaring.

Karex, which supplies condoms to brands such as Durex and Trojan, warned that disruption caused by the Strait of Hormuz’s closure has already led to planned price increases of between 20pc and 30pc.

However, it said this could increase further if the Middle East conflict drags on and the crucial trade route remains closed.

Most condoms are made of latex, which is a form of rubber produced using petrochemicals from Middle East supply chains.

The price of rubber has jumped 8.5pc in the last month, fuelled by increases in the price of oil.

Goh Miah Kiat, Karex’s chief executive, said the company had “no choice” but to pass costs on to customers.

“The situation is definitely very fragile, prices are expensive,” he said.

The closure of the Strait, which has been effectively shut for two months and is still subject to a blockade by the US navy, is expected to pile further pressure on consumer goods reliant on petrochemicals.

Karex, which sells five billion condoms each year, said that it had seen costs increase across its supply chain.

Packaging materials and lubricants – such as silicone oil and aluminium foil – had also risen in price.

Mr Kiat said Karex had enough condoms stockpiled to meet demand for the next few months, and would boost output to meet demand caused by US government funding cuts for foreign aid.

Last year, Donald Trump’s administration cut funding of the US Agency for International Development by 92pc, equivalent to $54bn (£43bn). It said the cuts were part of an “America First review of spending”.

Mr Kiat said demand for condoms had surged by 30pc this year, and warned of shortages caused by shipping disruptions.

The Malaysian company’s shipments to Europe and the US are now taking close to two months to arrive, twice as long as before Mr Trump’s war in Iran.

“We’re seeing a lot more condoms actually sitting on vessels that have not ⁠arrived ​at their destination but are highly required,” he added.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: UK airlines call for emergency jet fuel plan to avert summer chaos
data.description:
Britain’s airlines have called on the Government to present an emergency jet fuel plan or risk a summer of holiday chaos.

Airlines UK said ministers must prepare for fuel shortages now if disruption to flights is to be avoided. The association’s members include British Airways, Virgin Atlantic and easyJet.

It came as Lufthansa, Germany’s biggest airline, cancelled 20,000 flights in one of the biggest cuts of the crisis so far. The cuts to short-haul routes within Europe will last from May until October and save the airline 40,000 metric tons of jet fuel.

ACI Europe, which represents airports, has warned that some hubs may begin to run short of jet fuel by the middle of May, following the closure of vital supply lines during the Iran war.

Airlines UK urged Britain to bolster fuel reserves by forcing refineries to produce more kerosene and temporarily allowing US-grade fuel, which can be used by some planes, to be imported.

It called for Labour to cushion cost increases with measures including tax cuts and the suspension of stringent environmental regulations imposed on carriers.

The group warned about the “immediate impact on the UK aviation sector and UK consumers in the event disruption to jet fuel supply continues or worsens”.

Airlines UK’s demands, made in a paper sent to ministers and the Civil Aviation Authority, will be seen as an indication of the level of concern among airlines as they face dwindling fuel reserves. The Strait of Hormuz, which has been blockaded during the Middle East conflict, was a vital shipping route for 20pc of the world’s oil and gas volumes.

The paper, seen by The Telegraph, warned that costs for airlines were already rising faster in the UK than on the Continent before the Gulf crisis. The industry group pointed to increases in the air passenger duty departure tax and the imposition of a tougher mandate for the use of expensive sustainable aviation fuel.

It added: “Doubling of jet fuel costs – with fuel around one third of airline costs – represents a major, additional price shock.”

UK must ‘take right actions now’

Among other demands, Airlines UK called for an update of the “national emergency plan for fuel” to set out allocation plans for aviation fuel, not just petrol and diesel.

It said airlines should also be exempted from passenger compensation rules governing flight cancellations – not only when fuel runs short but also if they modify timetables to reflect changes in demand.

A spokesman for Airlines UK said: “It is vital that the Government takes the right actions now to ensure the continuation of supply, as well as support the UK aviation industry which has been impacted by record high jet fuel prices.

“Additional flexibility, should it be needed, would enable airlines to operate most efficiently to minimise any disruption to customers and maintain long-established global networks.”

BA’s owner, International Airlines Group, said its carriers were “not immune” to the jump in fuel costs even with a hedging strategy which provided near-term protection from price rises.

A spokesman said: “If these pressures continue, flexibility from government would ensure airlines can continue to operate as efficiently as possible and manage sustained cost challenges while keeping people and trade moving.”

Apostolos ⁠Tzitzikostas, the EU transport commissioner, said on Tuesday that guidance was being prepared for EU airlines on how to handle airport slots, passenger rights and public ​service obligations in the ‌event of fuel shortages.

The ‌commission is ‌due to present ⁠a broader package of energy and transport measures on Wednesday.

Airlines UK said: “If the EU provides guidance on some of these critical areas, we need the UK to set out where they too sit as soon as possible.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: London is becoming a Potemkin city
data.description:
What is happening to our cities? Physically, they are being hollowed out, as old buildings are eviscerated: their hinderparts completely destroyed and replaced, leaving only a thin veneer of original façade to the street. What a metaphor for a society that, in the political and money-making departments, seems devoid of principle.

Seeming survivals of old London, Manchester or Glasgow are often in reality no such thing. A fig leaf covers an obscenity of enormous proportions. Peek behind the frontage when the site is under construction and you will see a Goldfinger’s lair, the urban equivalent of the arch villain’s Kentucky stud farm in the 1964 film. Not only will the whole city block have been cleared but ground will have been dug out to sink many levels of basement into the bowels of the earth.

Goldfinger himself – I refer to the Modernist architect Ernő Goldfinger, not his fictional namesake – would have been horrified. Honesty to structure was a sacred tenet to followers of the so-called International Style, which arrived from the Continent in the 1930s. Applied decoration was anathema.

Propagandists for the style borrowed the language of A.W.N. Pugin, the Victorian architect. He had put welly into the Gothic Revival by making it a moral cause. Pointed (i.e. medieval) architecture was structurally consistent, Christian and pure; places such as Ramsgate, where, being a contrarian, he had chosen to live, were debased by stucco that hid shoddy brickwork and was painted to look like stone.

After the Second World War, the tenets of Modernism went unchallenged. However much ordinary people were attached to terraced streets, the only morally valid building technology was that which used steel, concrete and glass. The march of progress had ordained it. Pastiche was verboten.

Although the collapse of a new 22-storey tower called Ronan Point following a gas explosion in 1968 revealed (literal) cracks in the premise, the mood among developers and planners did not change until 1984, when the then Prince Charles articulated what many of the public were already saying to each other in his famous Carbuncle speech. The Prince had read David Watkin’s Morality and Architecture, published in 1977, which showed that the intellectual foundations of Modernism were piffle.

This created a problem. If Modernism fell foul of conservationists, whose voice was now being heard, how were developers to provide the sort of office buildings their clients wanted, whilst also making oodles of money? They couldn’t simply flatten pre-existing streets as they had done before.

The answer? Façadism. Keep the fronts and fool the public into believing that Dickens or Boswell might have strolled past and found nothing amiss, while building a completely new frame at the back. This became established practice wherever historic building considerations raised their heads. Conservation areas have been, in many cases, turned into shells as a consequence.

It is – no surprise – all driven by money, not only the desire to avoid costly delays in the planning process but the iniquitous Treasury policy of charging VAT on works to old buildings while giving a free pass to new construction. It works like this: developers propose to knock down a listed building; general horror erupts; they then accept a compromise which involves keeping the façade while pocketing the VAT exemption because, after all, the result will be 99 per cent new.

This scam offends as the holiest of 21st-century objectives: the reduction of carbon emissions. Old buildings lock up large quantities of embedded carbon, whereas new ones are massive emitters; so it’s better to work with old structures as far as possible.

I will admit that façadism does have one small virtue: it keeps the street line intact. How grateful we were then, as Britain emerged blinking from the Modernist hegemony, for that small saving grace. Streets are essential for happiness. They are where people meet each other. Visitors can instantly “read” them, seeing where the shops are, finding the front doors.

Stand-alone Modernist buildings were surrounded by soulless piazzas, where rubbish drifted like tumbleweed. Now, incredibly, numerous architects are working with the street, not against it. Not just traditionalists such as ADAM Architecture and Ben Pentreath, in the Charles III camp, but young firms such as Peter Barber Architects and Trevor Morriss of SPPARC.

On an unintended drive around South London earlier this week, after being misdirected by sat nav, I saw many examples. Come on guys, we can afford more self-belief. Keep old buildings intact, build new ones that make decent streets. Our cities deserve it.

data.title: Net zero zealots are putting Gen Z off building planes
data.description:
Britain’s aerospace companies are gearing up for their biggest boom in a generation.

Record airliner orders will require plane makers and their suppliers to lift production by a fifth in the next few years, creating well-paid jobs for thousands of workers.

Yet a UK aerospace industry that produced the jet engine, the Harrier jump jet and Concorde is being hampered by a growing recruitment crisis.

Years of demonisation by green campaigners such as Greta Thunberg for its inability to wean itself off fossil fuels are beginning to take their toll as young people turn away from careers in industry.

A lack of inspirational new aircraft and the allure of rival jobs in technology and AI are compounding the situation, leaving even the biggest firms struggling to hire.

Airbus, which employs 15,000 people in the UK, is resorting to converting one of its Beluga wing transporters into a recruitment centre in an effort to bolster its appeal to Gen Z.

The firm will fit the plane with classrooms and a mock-up of its assembly line as it seeks to recruit hundreds of apprentices a year to the Broughton wing plant in North Wales.

Bosses are hoping that Nasa’s Artemis programme to return humans to the Moon by 2028 will also help restore some of the wider industry’s lost glamour.

Richard Gardner, the honorary president of Farnborough Air Sciences Trust, said Gen Z had been turned off aviation by eco-zealots who wrongly condemn the sector for its environmental impact.

He suggested the problem starts in school, where children are increasingly taught about the impacts of climate change.

He said: “We talk about the word brainwashing, which is quite emotive for some people, but that is what it is.

“The teaching unions are very influential in terms of the way that profession is going, and they are pretty green in their tendencies.”

Gardner added: “I think they mostly buy into these green policies, science and economics teachers aside, and that aerospace and defence are therefore not high in their career recommendations.”

He said the recent rise of the Green Party as an electoral force is also a “big worry” for aviation.

Green policies include a commitment to banning domestic flights for journeys that could be made by train, halting airport expansion and imposing a tax on frequent travellers.

Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS, the trade group for aerospace, defence and space firms, said that while the industry might not be decarbonising fast enough for some young people, its focus on reducing emissions may be failing to motivate others.

He said: “There is a feeling that aerospace has faded into the background a bit because there are no longer any mountains being climbed.

“Concorde, the Comet, the jet engine and the Harrier were all British firsts that really excited and inspired people.”

Craven added: “All new aircraft are really a cylinder with wings and look approximately the same. There are incremental advances which are huge for sustainability, but it’s hard to see that progression.

“There is also huge competition from other high-tech arenas – particularly AI – and, more generally, the City, where vast swathes of money are available to the best and brightest.”

Mr Craven said it was galling to see tech companies recruiting the most able engineers, even though their electricity consumption is set to double by 2030, creating clear parallels with the net zero challenges facing aviation.

He said: “When you look at cloud computing, on the other side of it is a dirty big data centre requiring huge amounts of power and huge amounts of cooling.”

‘Incredibly competitive world’

Sue Partridge, the UK country manager for Airbus, said the world’s biggest plane maker had turned to initiatives such as the Beluga recruitment project as it prepares to deliver 1,000 jet planes a year as early as 2027, up from 793 in 2025.

Airbus’s Filton, Gloucestershire, wing design and development centre near Bristol faces competition for A-level students and graduate recruits from a broad spectrum of rival employers, she said.

The firm, which has trained 4,000 British recruits in the past decade, also faces a fight to hire 16-year-old apprentices for its assembly line in Broughton, Flintshire, with Merseyside’s carmakers and the cluster of green energy firms on nearby Deeside, Wales.

She said: “It’s an incredibly competitive world out there, and we need to do everything we can to attract talent and secure those skills we are going to need for the future.

“But it is difficult, particularly in newer digital skill areas, where we are competing with Google and companies like that, which was never the case in the past.”

Partridge added: “With the younger cohort, the challenge is attracting them to the industry in the first place and inspiring them so that they want to actually build aeroplanes. They get influenced in their career choice at an early age.

“A few years ago, all the stories were about flight shaming, and aerospace was the bad guy. That’s why we want to get into schools so they really connect with Airbus and aerospace manufacturing.”

The Beluga, named after the white whale whose bloated fuselage it resembles, garners rare attention for a modern plane and would be a formidable tool in encouraging take-up of Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) subjects, Ms Partridge said.

“There are still inspirational stories we can tell, and I think the Beluga is one,” she said. “We know people love the plane, and we can use that as a symbol.

“It’s an iconic aircraft, and if you see one in the sky, for me, it’s just as impactful as Concorde used to be. We already have a Concorde near Filton, South Gloucestershire, and we can use this in the same way.”

Mr Craven said the development of so-called flying taxis, in which Britain is playing a leading role, is one area that should resonate with younger people.

That is largely “because they will be silent, and there won’t be obvious pollutants pouring out of the back”.

However, it is not clear how big the market for the electric vertical take-off craft will be, with some industry observers concerned that they will be limited to whisking the very rich to and from first-class flights, a role hardly likely to endear them to Gen Z.

Industry experts hope that a new era of manned space travel could achieve greater cut-through.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has made space a “much closer frontier”, while the Artemis missions have the potential to capture hearts and minds just as the Apollo programme did in the 1960s and 1970s, said Mr Craven.

He said: “Aerospace and space are joined at the hip, and whenever we ask an astronaut to an event, there is a frisson of excitement. People want heroes, and that combination of high-tech and courage is the ultimate inspiration.

“I can remember watching the Moon landing on a black and white TV with a 10-inch screen in the middle of Africa. It’s probably why I ended up as an engineer.”

data.title: Shoplifting surges by 133pc in five years
data.description:
The number of shoplifting offences has risen by 133 per cent in the past five years, official figures show.

The number of thefts reported to police rose from 228,128 in 2020-21 to 530,457 in 2024-25, according to House of Commons Library data analysed by the Liberal Democrats.

However, only 19.83 per cent of offences in 2024-25 resulted in a charge, with wide variations between the 43 police forces in England and Wales.

At the bottom of the table was the Metropolitan Police, with fewer than 7 per cent of recorded cases resulting in a charge.

This was down from 8.64 per cent in 2020-21, when there were 31,008 shoplifting offences reported to the force. Since then, the number of store thefts has tripled to 93,626.

Durham Constabulary brought charges in 32.7 per cent of its shoplifting cases, more than four times the rate of the Met in 2024-25.

It was followed at the top of the table by Humberside (31.9 per cent), Norfolk (30.6 per cent), Cumbria (30.44 per cent) and West Mercia (30.21 per cent).

Marks & Spencer has criticised the failure of the Government and police to get a grip on shoplifting, which the retailer claimed had seen attacks on its stores by thieves become “more brazen, more organised and more aggressive”.

Executives from the store wrote to Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, and Shabana Mahmood, the Home Secretary, demanding they do more to address the problem.

The Lib Dems said small businesses “simply cannot afford for this to continue” and called for a return to community policing with dedicated police counters.

‘Our communities deserve better’

Max Wilkinson, the Lib Dems’ home affairs spokesman, said: “Our communities deserve better than the surge of shoplifting that’s ransacking our local high streets. For years, the Conservatives quietly dismantled community policing and this Labour Government has failed to properly restore it.

“Retail workers have, in too many cases, been left to fend for themselves as hundreds of thousands of shoplifters get away scot-free. Our brilliant small businesses simply cannot afford for this to continue.”

He added: “Liberal Democrats have time and again said we need a visible neighbourhood police presence, with staffed police counters in every community and officers on the ground. Only then can we make our high streets safe for shopkeepers and shoppers.”

Labour has committed to ensuring every community has a named contactable officer, and pledged to put 13,000 extra police officers back on the streets by the end of this Parliament.

Its new crime and policing bill also aims to curb rising shoplifting by repealing the “low-value” theft rule, which treats shoplifted items below £200 as minor summary offences. It also introduces a separate offence for assaulting retail workers and new respect orders to ban repeat offenders from town centres.

Earlier this week, the Met’s head of business crime insisted his force was determined to come down hard on shoplifting, having already nearly doubled detection rates, increased arrests of store thieves by 44 per cent and seen a 3.2 per cent drop in offences in London in the past year.

The Lib Dems have also raised concerns that shoplifting is particularly hitting small businesses, which are already under pressure from rising energy bills, business rates and extra costs from the Chancellor’s National Insurance contribution rise.

The party has called for a 5p VAT cut for hospitality businesses and urgent reform of the business rates system to breathe life into high streets.

A Home Office spokesman said: “This Government inherited unacceptable levels of shop theft. Real progress is being made, with the number of people caught and charged rising by 21 per cent.

“Alongside major policing reforms to boost neighbourhood patrols, supported by 13,000 extra neighbourhood officers, we are giving police the powers they need to tackle shop theft, including scrapping the £200 rule that lets too many offenders off the hook.”

data.title: Middle classes risk paying more for their shopping under surge pricing
data.description:
Middle-class households may have to pay more for their shopping as retailers roll out surge pricing, Bank of England economists have warned.

New AI-pricing systems may charge better-off households more in order to subsidise lower prices for poorer families, researchers wrote on the Bank’s blog.

The rise of algorithmic and electronic pricing means retailers are increasingly able to personalise prices for different customers, based on characteristics such as what they can afford or particularly need.

Anthony Savagar, Misa Tanaka and Jagdish Tripathy, all Bank of England economists, wrote: “This may lead to higher price dispersion as individuals with high willingness-to-pay subsidise those with lower willingness-to-pay.”

In practice, this is likely to mean better-off households paying more for items than those who are worse off.

Retailers could charge different prices depending on where a person lives. Research in the US shows that shops there already do this.

Mr Savagar wrote: “With increased availability of big data and computing power, more firms are adopting algorithmic and AI-powered pricing to adjust prices rapidly in response to changing economic conditions over time and across consumers.”

Clare Lombardelli, a senior Bank of England official, warned earlier this month of “concerns about fairness” as surge pricing could soon be coming to supermarkets.

A report in February by think tank Onward found that Britons were already spending £10bn a year on subsidies for people claiming Universal Credit through discounts on services for benefit claimants.

These include reduced broadband tariffs, council tax discounts and prescription charge exemptions, which are subsidised by the people paying the full costs.

Households living on benefits can also get major discounts on experiences such as visits to London Zoo, Westminster Abbey and the Tower of London, where a family of four can get a £107 discount worth 96pc of their ticket price.

The rise of big data and technology such as electronic shelf labels means that retailers are increasingly using AI algorithms to adjust prices rapidly based on supply and demand.

Businesses are also exploring personalised pricing, where they tailor prices to each individual customer, based on their circumstances or interests. In practice, this could mean charging a person more for a band T-shirt if streaming data showed they were one of the group’s biggest fans.

Mr Savagar warned that the rise of electronic and algorithmic systems could also make inflation shocks more intense as prices rise almost immediately.

Price surges could also become steeper because the algorithms could push shops to “raise markups more aggressively when inflation is already high”, he said.

data.title: The man who built AirPods must now lead Apple into the AI era
data.description:
When Tim Cook took over at Apple in 2011, it was a company in mourning. One of his first jobs was arranging a private memorial service for employees to honour Apple’s mercurial co-founder Steve Jobs.

At the time, many questioned whether the softly spoken supply-chain guru from Alabama was the right man to inherit Jobs’ legacy. In his tribute, Cook said some of the last advice Jobs gave him – after nominating him as the next chief executive – was to “never ask what he would do, just do what’s right”.

Now, 15 years later, Jobs’ choice has largely been vindicated. Apple has been transformed from a pioneering – and often turbulent – California gadget business into a $4tn (£2.3tn) global juggernaut.

Cook will now be looking forward to a happier, smoother handover to his own hand-picked successor: Apple’s hardware chief John Ternus. The transition is planned for September, alongside a new iPhone launch. Cook will step up to become the company’s chairman.

By his own admission, Cook was not the technological visionary that Jobs was. Nor was he a design obsessive like Sir Jony Ive. Yet during his tenure, Apple shifted from a device-maker that catered to a small number of die-hard fans into a company that sells 300 million new gadgets each year.

It also released two new gadgets that have become multibillion-dollar businesses in their own right – the Apple Watch and AirPods.

As Cook steps aside, Apple is turning to its top engineering talent – who took those products from ideas to reality – to navigate the artificial intelligence era.

At 50, Ternus is among the youngest members of Apple’s senior leadership team – and about as old as the business itself. He started at Apple in 2001 working on its screen technology and took over as senior vice-president of hardware engineering in 2013.

“Having spent almost my entire career at Apple, I have been lucky to have worked under Steve Jobs and to have had Tim Cook as my mentor,” Ternus said on Monday.

Cook said Ternus had the “mind of an engineer, the soul of an innovator, and the heart to lead with integrity and honour”.

While he is little known beyond the tech world, Ternus has played a role in developing every new Apple gadget of the past two decades. He led its internal chip innovations and worked on the hardware development of multiple models of MacBook, iPad and AirPods.

Since 2020, he has also been in charge of its iPhone hardware division. Most recently, his team launched the MacBook Neo, which has proved an instant hit.

Apple’s announcement praised his work driving “advancements in AirPods to make them the world’s best in-ear headphones”. The AirPod line was first launched in 2016, three years after Ternus took over as top engineer.

Internally, Ternus has been primed to take charge. He has been given a public-facing role at major Apple keynotes and been handed responsibility over the company’s product roadmap.

Yet now he must also grapple with the crucial question of how Apple responds to the rise of AI.

So far, the company has been seen as a laggard. ChatGPT has made Apple’s Siri look increasingly dumb. Its Apple Intelligence upgrade fell flat and led it to launch some AI apps that were uncharacteristically buggy and unpopular.

A big unknown is whether Ternus, whose CV speaks to his expertise in crafting exquisite hardware, can steer Apple’s AI ambitions.

“Given his background in hardware, I don’t know if he [Ternus] is the right person to work out where Apple fits in the new AI landscape,” says Richard Windsor, an independent technology analyst.

So far, Apple has sat out of the race to build AI super intelligence. Four years after the launch of ChatGPT, Siri has seen very little change.

Yet some analysts now see this as a deliberate choice. Apple’s main rivals are expected to spend $700bn this year on AI infrastructure. Some are taking on tens of billions in debt.

While AI apps from the likes of OpenAI have garnered much attention, not all have been successful. OpenAI recently shut down its Sora video editing tool after a few months, having burnt billions in the process.

Meanwhile, Apple’s financial might is in no doubt. The company has $150bn in cash, and its iPhone sales have proved resilient, hitting a new record in 2025.

“The business models of AI have yet to resonate, especially for consumers,” Horace Dediu, a US technology analyst, wrote last month. AI chatbots are also becoming increasingly commoditised despite vast investment by labs. “Apple didn’t miss the AI revolution. It just bet that the winners won’t be the ones who build the infrastructure.”

Instead of pursuing OpenAI or Google in making the next bot, Ternus’s appointment could signal Apple is betting that top-of-the-range hardware will be all the more important in the AI age.

“Ternus is a hardware engineer, which signals that Apple will seek differentiation in its physical products,” says Dipanjan Chatterjee, an analyst at Forrester.

Indeed, Dediu argues that its superpowered chips give it a technological head start on making AI work on phones, watches or headsets.

Apple has already seen an explosion of demand for its technology from AI coders wanting the best machines to run their apps.

Its smallest Mac Mini computers have enjoyed a massive jump in interest, and are widely sold out.

It has also reportedly been working on new smart glasses, an AI pendant, and new AirPod-like headphones with a camera. These would take advantage of new Siri capabilities, according to Bloomberg. Apple could reveal its AI spectacles before the end of the year.

Alongside these expected launches, Apple is rumoured to be gearing up to unveil a folding version of the iPhone later this year. It would be the biggest design change for the handset since it was first unveiled almost 20 years ago.

‘I would bet on Apple keeping its place’

With OpenAI and Meta both experimenting with new AI gadgets, Ben Thompson, the veteran technology analyst, argues that failing to win the next round of hardware innovation is Apple’s “real AI threat”, rather than the current chatbot wars.

Today, the smartphone remains the most-used device globally. “If ever a better interaction paradigm were to succeed the smartphone, surely it will be rooted in AI – and Apple, by giving up now, won’t be in the game,” Thompson says. “This absolutely is not a prediction. Indeed, if I had to bet, I would bet on Apple keeping its place.”

After 50 years of Apple – where it has become one of the most profitable companies in the world – the new era of AI represents a pivotal moment for the tech firm. It will be down to Ternus to steer Apple through this revolution.

Cook says his successor is more than ready for the challenge. He said Ternus was, after all, a “visionary”.

data.title: Duolingo in running to test migrant English skills for Home Office
data.description:
Duolingo is lining up to run migrant English language tests for the Home Office, The Telegraph can reveal.

The US app is closing in on a five-year, £816m contract to conduct language tests for foreign workers, students and other migrants seeking entry to Britain.

The Home Office has been assessing initial bids and will undertake technical assessments of companies in May. Best and final offers from companies in the running are expected over the summer and the Home Office plans to award the contract by November.

Duolingo is among the bidders for the lucrative contract, though there is no guarantee it will win the work.

Foreign visa applicants currently sit exams overseas with trained invigilators to prove they have a minimum level of English language skill.

Labour has been seeking to scrap the current system and replace it with an all-digital testing programme. Shabana Mahmood, the Home Secretary, has also sought to strengthen entry requirements by demanding higher levels of English from migrants.

Duolingo, known for its free smartphone app that provides language courses, has offered standardised online English testing since 2014.

Its timed tests gained popularity during the pandemic as assessment centres shut down. They are already being used by some UK universities as part of their applications process for foreign students.

While universities are allowed to set their own English tests for potential students, Duolingo is not currently approved for the “secure English language test”, the Home Office’s main immigration assessment.

Ms Mahmood’s plans to scrap the current in-person language testing system and make it online-only have attracted criticism from established providers, who warned of the risk of widespread cheating.

The International English Language Testing System (IELTS) consortium, which handles more than two thirds of UK assessments, wrote to Ms Mahmood in March to warn that online tests would result in “weaker security and more opportunities for malpractice” in the immigration system.

Challenges of remote testing

IELTS, a consortium made up of the British Council, Cambridge University Press & Assessment and education company IDP, dropped out of the bidding for the new online contract last month over its concerns about the plan.

“Evidence from research and regulatory practice shows that fully remote testing presents challenges in meeting these standards consistently – especially in the highest stakes environments where security is paramount,” the IELTS board said.

The group has warned that applicants could use AI to cheat the tests. Fraudsters could also use the technology to impersonate candidates, it added.

Duolingo has introduced ID checks for its language tests and uses eye tracking to check if students are trying to use crib sheets or AI.

Winning the Home Office contract would be a boost for Duolingo. Shares in the company, known for its green owl mascot, have collapsed 70pc in 12 months over concerns that new AI-powered language apps and translation services could hurt its user numbers.

Duolingo said in November that more than 50 million people globally were using its app each day.

In response to a parliamentary question this week on whether officials had held talks with Duolingo about the testing scheme, Mike Tapp, the immigration minister, said: “Prior to the Home Office English language testing programme publishing invitation to tender, five rounds of market engagement were conducted under non-disclosure agreements.

“However, there were opportunities for those who participated to share information that they wished for the Home Office to consider and ask clarifying questions.

“It would not be appropriate to disclose details of individual organisations’ participation in a confidential market engagement process.”

A government spokesman said: “The Home Office English language test is in live procurement and has not yet been awarded.” 

A Duolingo spokesman declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: South East Water boss waives bonus after ‘getting it wrong’ on supply crisis
data.description:
The boss of South East Water has admitted that he “got it wrong” during outages in Tunbridge Wells as he gave up his bonus over the disruption.

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of the embattled utility company, said he had made mistakes during the incident, which left thousands of homes without water for weeks. It resulted in schools in Kent closing and elderly residents queuing for two hours for bottled water.

Mr Hinton said he had “got it wrong” over how he dealt with the public. Chris Train, the company’s chairman, confirmed that Mr Hinton would not be taking his £115,000 bonus for this year.

He received a £400,000 basic salary and a £115,000 bonus last year, as well as being paid a further £50,000 for working overtime.

The chief executive was notably absent during the crisis, which began in November 2025. About 24,000 homes and businesses across the South East lost drinking water for two weeks.

Mr Hinton refused to speak to the media during the outages and attacked Mike Martin, a local MP, for having “politicised the issue”.

‘We accept that we failed’

However, appearing before MPs on the Environment Committee on Tuesday, Mr Hinton admitted the company’s response had been “disappointing” and said mistakes were made in how quickly he communicated with the public.

He said: “That is very much a lesson that we have learnt for the playbook of how we handle future events.”

Mr Hinton said South East Water “should have reacted on those earlier warnings quicker, and our problem-solving around it when we got into that situation was too slow and unstructured”.

Mr Train, the non-executive chairman of the utility company, said: “We accept that we failed in our primary duty and we could have done better.”

The admission follows an earlier hearing when Mr Hinton had sought to defend himself before the Environment Committee. During the first appearance, he told MPs that the outage, which was caused by the failure of a water treatment plant, was not foreseeable.

He rated himself eight out of 10 for his response, claiming that “lifestyle changes”, including more people working from home, were partly to blame for the incident.

‘Cat with nine live’

The earlier hearing came the same day as fresh disruption hit the Tunbridge Wells area. On Jan 7, the taps ran dry again in Tunbridge Wells because of thawing pipes cracking. It affected 25,000 homes in the area.

On Tuesday, Mr Train would not be drawn on whether there would be any failure sufficient that he would ask Mr Hinton to step down.

Henry Tufnell, an MP who sits on the Environment Committee, told The Telegraph: “Hinton’s capacity to survive is remarkable. He’s like a cat with nine lives.”

Mr Tufnell added: “We used to pride ourselves on being able to turn on the tap [to have] high-quality drinking water. To have lost that and to have lost that trust and confidence is just shocking and quite damning.”

data.title: Why Britain faces the growing risk of Spanish-style blackouts
data.description:
The great British blackout was once a threat that hung over the country during the depths of winter, when generators struggled to meet demand or storms destroyed power lines.

But today, as the net zero revolution rolls on, the greatest threat to grid stability could land on a sunny Sunday afternoon.

According to the National Energy System Operator (Neso), the body responsible for keeping the lights on, the UK power grid is at increasing risk of being overwhelmed by surges in solar power.

Underpinning this threat is the fact that too many solar panels have been connected to the grid without any way of monitoring them or controlling their output.

As a result, a typically sunny day in summer can lead to nearly 19 gigawatts (GW) of power pouring into the UK’s networks, while only 12-15GW is being consumed.

That imbalance is a recipe for disaster because significant destabilisation of the grid can cause blackouts.

Such a calamity struck Spain and Portugal last year, when a chain reaction triggered by solar farms knocked out power to 55 million people.

At the time, solar and wind generated 80pc of energy in the Iberian Peninsula, the result of renewables displacing power stations run by nuclear and gas.

While a welcome switch for supporters of green energy, the removal of traditional power stations can prove problematic – as Spain and Portugal discovered last year.

This is because of their importance in providing vital system stability, known as inertia.

Inertia is created by generators with spinning parts – such as turbines running on gas, coal or hydropower – which wind and solar do not have.

This form of kinetic energy helps to prevent sudden changes in a system’s frequency.

Therefore, when two solar farms suddenly disconnected from Spain’s grid last year, there was no steady inertia to keep the country’s power networks afloat.

In practice, Neso is confident the UK will never see such a disaster. However, it admits the challenge is growing as ever more solar farms are built in Ed Miliband’s net zero drive.

“We are confident that we have the tools required to operate the system safely and reliably,” Neso said in its Summer Outlook report.

“But there may be periods when balancing supply, demand and system needs will require us to use our full range of operational tools, including system notices.”

However, independent experts are not so sure – and we may soon find out who is right. The crunch will probably come around the late May bank holiday, or on any sunny summer Sunday.

Last year, that date saw national demand for power plummet to a record low of 11GW, while sun and wind drove up power generation to nearly 19GW. And crucially, Neso could do little to stop it.

This sense of powerlessness is new. In the past, the grid operator’s response to an excess of power was simply to switch off some power stations.

But Neso’s problem is that it can no longer see what is happening on the UK’s power networks.

Unlike before, energy is being created naturally from wind and solar farms that Neso cannot control.

Underlying Britain’s energy grid are two main power networks.

At the top is the high-voltage transmission grid, which takes electricity from power stations to cities, towns and industry.

Everything on it is closely measured and monitored from Neso’s Electricity National Control Centre in Wokingham, Berkshire.

There, controllers use a giant wall-to-wall digital map of the transmission network to match supply to demand – switching power stations on or reducing output as needed.

Below that lies the distribution network, which takes electricity from the transmission grid, reduces its voltage, and pumps it into our homes and businesses.

It was designed to be a passive power carrier, meaning it lacks most of the switching or measuring systems found on the transmission grid. Ultimately, this makes it hard to monitor or control.

However, in the rush for decarbonisation, officials have been hooking the distribution system up to hundreds of new power sources, particularly solar farms.

Almost all of those solar generators are largely invisible to Neso’s operators, and they cannot see how much power is being generated or switch it off.

This now poses a problem, according to Simon Gallagher, the chief executive of UK Network Services, a leading consultancy.

“Traditionally, most power generators were connected to the transmission system and ‘dispatched’ centrally, so Neso can instruct them to turn on, turn off, ramp up and down to balance the grid in real time,” he says.

“Big power stations with large gas generators get tuned up and down as Neso monitors changes in demand.

“But solar is almost all connected to the distribution system and Neso has no control or even visibility of what it is doing. It means there is a risk of imbalances. On a sunny summer Sunday, demand will be very low, but solar will be generating at full capacity.”

However, whether this results in a blackout similar to Spain is another matter.

Some industry experts say an energy crisis is unlikely in the UK because its grid is structured differently, but regional failures and high costs remain a threat.

“Britain’s solar surplus problem is more about balancing costs than sudden system failure,” says Timothy Santonastaso, the deputy head of European power pricing at the consultancy Argus.

Such problems should have been addressed sooner, according to Claire Coutinho, the energy secretary in the last Conservative government and now a shadow minister.

‘Bogus numbers’

While in power, Coutinho says she became so worried about the impact of excessive solar disrupting the grid that she ordered civil servants to investigate.

This involved a review of the costs associated with balancing the grid when it is too windy or sunny.

Last year, the Government spent more than £1bn on paying wind farms to switch off in so-called curtailment costs.

“I started a full systems cost of wind and solar in the department because we need to know the true costs of renewables to consumers,” she says.

“I asked officials to prove to me that renewables were cheap and then realised their numbers were bogus.”

Coutinho claims Miliband has since cancelled her review. She also says the Energy Secretary is paying too much for net zero.

A spokesman for Miliband rejected the criticisms, instead claiming that solar and wind farms would bring huge benefits. “We are reversing decades of underinvestment and delivering the biggest upgrade to the grid in decades, which will minimise constraint costs, meet the capacity needed to deliver clean power by 2030 and help bring down bills for good,” he said.

He also played down the threat of grid disruption. “The National Energy System Operator has always had to manage changes in demand between the winter and summer months. They have well-established tools to manage the grid, including during times of low demand, which will not impact supply.”

The fresh row over solar is just the latest front in the battle over green energy.

Within Labour, Miliband’s renewables rollout has delighted the party faithful and made him increasingly popular among members.

However, it has also turned energy and climate policy, an area on which there was general political agreement until two years ago, into a culture war.

Leading the charge against Labour is Reform UK, which has pledged to disconnect solar farms if it wins power at the next general election.

“These solar farms are being built for the benefit of foreign investors, but they are destroying our countryside and ruining our environment,” says Richard Tice, Reform’s energy spokesman.

“It’s my living mission to stop this madness. Britain has done its bit – we’ve cut emissions by 50pc and impoverished ourselves doing it. Now’s the time to make Britain prosperous again, and that means cheap energy, lots of it, whether from gas, nuclear or oil.”

No doubt Miliband will disagree. However, the latest warning from Neso may at least make him sit up and notice.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Reeves promises help on soaring factory energy bills – but only in a year’s time
data.description:
British factories face a year-long wait for relief from soaring energy bills despite Rachel Reeves’s suggestion that help is on the way.

The Chancellor promised on Sunday to “set out the next phase of our plans to boost Britain’s competitiveness” in response to high energy costs, which are worsening because of the war in Iran.

But The Telegraph understands that one of the Government’s key measures to cut the country’s crippling industrial energy bills cannot start any earlier than April 2027.

Business groups urged Ms Reeves to move faster, with a wave of inflation already hitting companies. New surveys of confidence and output suggest both have already fallen to lows not seen since the pandemic.

Addressing the “global economic crisis” triggered by war Iran, the Chancellor wrote over the weekend that she would “set out the principles that will guide how we support businesses in the months ahead”.

Ms Reeves is this week expected to outline details of the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS), which will see green levies on electricity costs waived for about 7,000 companies in eight key sectors.

However, a government source indicated that the Chancellor would not bring forward the timelines for the programme to launch. It means factories face a year-long wait for relief at a time when energy costs are soaring.

‘Essential’ to move faster

The Confederation of British Industry (CBI) lobby group said it was “essential” to move faster. “It needs to be delivered at pace. Any delay risks undermining business confidence at a time when it is already under strain,” said Tania Kumar, the CBI’s director for energy transition.

In a survey of 3,000 British voters conducted by the Institute for Economic Affairs, 77pc wanted the Government to focus on cutting the cost of energy. Similarly large majorities urged the Government to focus on economic growth, and cut taxes on workers and businesses.

In an article in The Sunday Times, the Chancellor said she did not want to be rushed into potentially expensive or ineffective policies.

Her hesitancy came as Donald Trump on Sunday said the US Navy would block the Strait of Hormuz – a vital transit route at least one fifth of the world’s oil, gas and fertiliser – to any ship that had paid a toll to Iran.

This could extend a conflict that has already squeezed energy supply chains and driven up fuel and electricity costs. Brent crude prices jumped as high as $104 a barrel on Monday morning after closing on Friday at $94.

Ian Stewart, the Deloitte chief economist, said the Iran war was “reshaping business sentiment” after a survey of finance chiefs by the consultancy found confidence at a six-year low. The barometer fell to -57pc in the first three months of this year, from minus 13pc the previous quarter.

Mr Stewart said: “It’s created a shock to CFO confidence, lowering optimism to levels we haven’t seen since the early days of the Covid-19 pandemic.

“Finance leaders are coping with high levels of external uncertainty, and their focus is on managing risks from geopolitics, rising energy prices and higher financing costs.”

A separate survey of 4,000 businesses by accountancy firm BDO found output had dropped to a five-year low.

“The decline in business output and confidence underlines the UK’s exposure to the global energy shock,” said Scott Knight, head of growth at BDO.

“Confidence will not be restored until energy flows stabilise under a lasting ceasefire.”

BDO warned the combination of increasing price pressures and falling demand would prompt businesses to stop hiring workers, with employment intentions at a 15-year low.

Britain’s manufacturers were already labouring under the highest electricity prices in Europe, but these will rise still further if the Iran conflict keeps squeezing oil and gas supplies.

Ms Kumar said the Chancellor was “right to resist the lure of short-term, unaffordable sticking plasters”, but she needed to seize this moment to make Britain more cost-competitive.

The BICS is a seven-year scheme to exempt businesses from paying two green levies and an energy-security charge. This could reduce their electricity costs by up to £40 per megawatt hour.

Scheme ‘does not go far enough’

To be eligible, factories must be in economically critical sectors. These include advanced manufacturing, clean energy, defence, digital technologies and life sciences. Suppliers to these sectors, such as makers of chemicals, steel or critical minerals, can also qualify.

Responding to a consultation on the BICS that ended in January, the Aluminium Federation said its members had “consistently reported that the proposed April 2027 implementation date is far too late”.

The Chemicals Industry Association told the Government that the scheme was not as generous as others in the EU, and “does not go far enough to make the UK a globally competitive manufacturing location”.

Other business groups supported the BICS but warned the government not to fund the scheme by increasing levies on companies that were not part of it.

Elexon, a company that provides data and settlement services to the energy industry, said getting BICS up and running by next April would be “very challenging”.

Its submission said the Government had to amend laws and build new processes and systems, and much of the groundwork for that would need to be finished by early this summer.

The Treasury declined to comment on speculation that Ms Reeves would this week announce an expansion of the BICS.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.
